title: From Control To Surrender
study: A Study In The Life Of King David
scriptureForWeek: 1 SAMUEL 21-27 These chapters cover David fleeing from Saul ever the course of several years and several incidents.
weekTheme: Boys panic, Men accept God will never leave us One of the big mistakes people can make in trying to follow Jesus in the world is believing that faith makes life easier. Not true! When you become a Christian, you wake up and realize that you are in a war. And in a war, there are enemies, threats, and dangers. You will experience resistance, pushback, and threats from other people who are intimidated by God's favor on your life or angry at what you believe. Following Jesus doesn’t remove your difficulties; it gives you strength and courage to get through them. As you continue on your journey to manhood, you are going to realize you will face this resistance. How a man learns to deal with resistance will determine how much God can trust him in his life. David had to learn this when dealing with King Saul. Saul knew that God had rejected him because he refused to truly surrender to him. Saul knew there was nothing he could do to keep his kingdom, no matter how hard he tried, so he got mad at the man God had chosen, David. And to frustrate Saul further, David only grew in power and popularity. This produced a favor backlash and made Saul freak out. He tried to cancel David in the most serious way possible - by killing him. David had to run, hide, and, at one point, even act crazy. Yet he surrenders his safety to God and learned to trust in him in the most unreal circumstances. David got familiar with change, uncertainty, and discomfort. In John 16:33, Jesus said, “I have told you these things, so that in me you may have peace. In this world you will have trouble. But take heart! I have overcome the world.” Your troubles may not be like David, where you have to flee for your actual life (even though many Christians in other parts of the world have

days.title: The Evil Of Envy
days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 19:8-18 Once more war broke out, and David went out and fought the Philistines. He struck them with such force that they fled before him. But an evil spirit from the LORD came on Saul as he was sitting in his house with his spear in his hand. While David was playing the lyre, Saul tried to pin him to the wall with his spear, but David eluded him as Saul drove the spear into the wall. That night David made good his escape. Saul sent men to David’s house to watch it and to kill him in the morning. But Michal, David’s wife, warned him, “If you don’t run for your life tonight, tomorrow you’ll be killed.” So Michal let David down through a window, and he fled and escaped. Then Michal took an idol and laid it on the bed, covering it with a garment and putting some goats’ hair at the head. When Saul sent the men to capture David, Michal said, “He is ill.” Then Saul sent the men back to see David and told them, “Bring him up to me in his bed so that I may kill him.” But when the men entered, there was the idol in the bed, and at the head was some goats’ hair. Saul said to Michal, “Why did you deceive me like this and send my enemy away so that he escaped?” Michal told him, “He said to me, ‘Let me get away. Why should I kill you?’” When David had fled and made his escape, he went to Samuel at Ramah and told him all that Saul had done to him. Then he and Samuel went to Naioth and stayed there.
days.reflection: Envy is a really bad sin that we don’t talk about that much these days. Envy can make us want what other people have. Envy can make us wish we were someone else. Envy can screw up a man's life really badly. Sometimes envy is just a passing thought and doesn’t do much damage. Maybe you have experienced this kind of thinking: “I wish I had his sneakers,” “I wish I was taller,” or “I wish I was more popular.” Those passing thoughts happen to us all, and it’s part of being human. But when envy really gets into a man’s heart, it can do all sorts of crazy stuff. Envy can make a man stalk his girlfriend. Envy can make a man lie about someone else to make themselves look good. Envy can even cause someone to kill someone else, like Cain killed his brother Abel in the Old Testament. When a man is gripped by envy, he has lost control of his life. King Saul has become envious of David. David is younger, braver, and more widely loved by the people. Saul’s fear of losing his power takes over his heart, and he tries to kill David. Something really interesting happens here. David doesn’t try and fight Saul, even though he may have been able to overpower him. David doesn’t start shouting the promises of God and quoting bible verses. David doesn’t pull his slingshot out and get busy. David sneaks off to safety. Look at what today's reading says: So Michal let David down through a window, and he fled and escaped. There is a time to be brave and confront, and there is a time to flee and escape. When you are dealing with someone who is gripped with a crazy desire and is not thinking rationally, you are in a dangerous situation. Rather than try to reason with a person like that or fight them, just get away from them. Sometimes the bravest and wisest thing to do is flee. Surrendering ourselves to God doesn’t mean we need to be stupid. We don’t want some fake kind of masculinity that thinks we always have to fight. The goal is to get through life skillfully and live to fight another day. David also went to Samuel the man of God who anointed him as king. He knew he would find encouragement and comfort in the presence of the prophet. You don’t want to run from your problems. You don’t want to run from God. You don’t want to run from something you need to confront. But you do want to run from crazy folks. If you get in a situation where someone is out of their mind, get out of there. David fled, Jesus had to escape, and you may too. There is no shame in that. Be wise, and don’t care what others think. That’s real strength.
questions: Have you ever been in a bad situation where someone was acting crazy? If so, what was it, and what happened?
What sort of things do guys your age tend to get jealous of? What sort of things have you been jealous of before?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time when they had to escape from a bad situation. What was it, how did they do it, and how was this the wise situation?

thoughts.verse: Pray, too, that we will be rescued from wicked and evil people, for not everyone is a believer. But the Lord is faithful; he will strengthen you and guard you from the evil one.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for his wisdom on how to handle difficult and dangerous situations. Ask him for wisdom in any areas you are facing problems now.

days.title: The Truth About Lies
days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 21:1-9 David went to Nob, to Ahimelek the priest. Ahimelek trembled when he met him, and asked, “Why are you alone? Why is no one with you?” David answered Ahimelek the priest, “The king sent me on a mission and said to me, ‘No one is to know anything about the mission I am sending you on.’ As for my men, I have told them to meet me at a certain place. Now then, what do you have on hand? Give me five loaves of bread, or whatever you can find.” But the priest answered David, “I don’t have any ordinary bread on hand; however, there is some consecrated bread here—provided the men have kept themselves from women.” David replied, “Indeed women have been kept from us, as usual whenever I set out. The men’s bodies are holy even on missions that are not holy. How much more so today!” So the priest gave him the consecrated bread, since there was no bread there except the bread of the Presence that had been removed from before the LORD and replaced by hot bread on the day it was taken away. Now one of Saul’s servants was there that day, detained before the LORD; he was Doeg the Edomite, Saul’s chief shepherd. David asked Ahimelek, “Don’t you have a spear or a sword here? I haven’t brought my sword or any other weapon, because the king’s mission was urgent.” The priest replied, “The sword of Goliath the Philistine, whom you killed in the Valley of Elah, is here; it is wrapped in a cloth behind the ephod. If you want it, take it; there is no sword here but that one.” David said, “There is none like it; give it to me.”
days.reflection: Is it ever ok to lie or distort the truth? Men have been asking this question since time began. On one hand, the bible says that we should not lie; we should speak the truth and not deceive our neighbors. It's even one of the 10 commandments (Number 9). But then you come across some tricky situations where godly people in the bible seem to clearly deceive others in certain situations. How can we make sense of this? The tension comes when related to saving lives and escaping danger. We know it’s wrong to deceive people to take advantage of them or cover up evil, but is it wrong to lie to save people? The most famous version of this question is posed in the following situation: “If it was WW2 and you were hiding Jewish people in your home, and Nazi soldiers knocked on your door and asked if you were hiding Jews, what would you do?” If you say yes, you and the Jewish people will be dragged off to the concentration camps and die. If you say no, the Jewish people will probably live and you will too. What is the godly thing to do here? King David faces one of these situations in his own life when fleeing from Saul. He does things that are normally wrong, but in this situation, they save his life. He lies about why he is in that part of the country. He takes things from the Tabernacle that are forbidden for regular men in regular situations to eat. And he takes Goliath's sword which is being kept there as a memorial of God's victory as a weapon for himself. David distorts the truth in a time of danger so that he can live. As you move out of childhood and into manhood, you will begin to realize things are more complex than they seem. Following God with maturity requires you to wrestle with what the bible teaches about God, but also wrestle with how those things are played out in real life. In answering this age-old question, Paul Carter writes, “The people of God are required to tell the truth, without equivocation or deceit, in every situation, except when doing so would result in the death of innocent people.” We can see this with the Hebrew midwives who were able to save Moses. Rahab the prostitute hid the Israelite spies and said they had left her house. David takes the break and makes up a mission to get supplies for himself and his men. Surrendering ourselves and our safety to God is more complex than it may initially appear. Sometimes God does miracles, sometimes you have to flee, and sometimes you have to frame things in a way that enables you to escape. That’s why it’s so important you become wise. That’s why it’s so important you keep learning and growing and not just wasting your time on entertainment and childish things that don’t help your life in the real world. God is training you to be a man who can navigate these kinds of complexities. So keep learning, and keep leaning in. You never know when you will find yourself in a moral dilemma that requires godly wisdom.
questions: How would you answer this age-old question? “If it was WW2, and you were hiding Jewish people in your, and Nazi soldiers knocked on your door and asked if you were hiding Jews, what would you do?
Ask the person you are doing this study with how they would respond in this situation.
Can you think of any situations in modern life where this approach may be necessary? Ask the person you are doing this study with if they can.

thoughts.verse: If you need wisdom, ask our generous God, and he will give it to you. He will not rebuke you for asking James 1:5 Read through his verse slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you wisdom on how to be a man of truth and how to be a man who cares for others.

days.title: Insane Survival
days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 21:10-15 That day David fled from Saul and went to Achish king of Gath. But the servants of Achish said to him, “Isn’t this David, the king of the land? Isn’t he the one they sing about in their dances: “‘Saul has slain his thousands, and David his tens of thousands’?” David took these words to heart and was very much afraid of Achish king of Gath. So he pretended to be insane in their presence; and while he was in their hands he acted like a madman, making marks on the doors of the gate and letting saliva run down his beard. Achish said to his servants, “Look at the man! He is insane! Why bring him to me? Am I so short of madmen that you have to bring this fellow here to carry on like this in front of me? Must this man come into my house?”
days.reflection: When we talk about the beauty of nature, sometimes people think of lovely sunsets, peaceful oceans, dolphins jumping in the air, eagles flying, or baby lambs saying hello. But rarely do people think about how harsh nature can be. We don’t think about the lions eating baby lambs, eagles eating fish, or the sun causing droughts. In response to the harshness of nature, animals have defense mechanisms. They adapt when threatened. Zebras have stripes. Cheetahs have spots. Pufferfish blow themselves up to look bigger than they are. Porcupines have quills. Skunks will spray you, and octopuses shoot ink. The need to adapt to survive is a key part of remaining safe. People have to learn to do this too, and sometimes they have to do some heavy things. In today's passage, things are not going well for David. King Saul is on the hunt for him, and he is so desperate that he goes into the land of the enemies. Can you imagine how wild this is? David killed Goliath and beat the Philistines because Saul called for a man willing to fight. Now David is having to run to the Philistines for protection because Saul is trying to kill him. Life can be filled with momentum swings that we never see coming. But when David gets there, he realizes he is in terrible danger. His reputation as a warrior is a liability in this situation. So, he does something shocking. He pretends to be insane. He dribbles all over himself and starts scrawling all over the doors and gates. David is doing whatever it takes to survive. The king thinks he has been hit in the head too many times in battle, believes David is insane, and lets him go. How humiliating for David, the true and anointed king of Israel to have to act this way. But it worked. David must have been a good actor as well as a warrior and musician because it saved his life. David was willing to adapt in a dangerous situation. He actually wrote a whole Psalm about this experience. It's Psalm 34 and it's one of the most beautiful in the bible. You too will face situations where you are out of your depths and get in real danger. It may be in high school, in college with a group of guys, or when you are older as a man. You will have to figure out what you need to do to adapt to the situation to survive. We are not talking about giving in to peer pressure here so that you can fit in and be liked by other people; we are talking about adapting for survival. You may need to do some stuff that feels embarrassing, weird, or hard. But if you stay connected to God in your heart, you won’t lose yourself in the situation. You will have an inner strength even if things get weird outside. In fact, why don’t you read Psalm 34 to see how David felt and how he relied on God? The Philistine king could never have known when David was acting crazy how trusting and surrendered David was in his heart. If you walk with God, you can have an inner source of strength no matter how much adapting you have to do in your life to make it through. Though it may feel kind of shameful at the time, if you look to him you will be radiant, and in God's eyes, you will never be covered in shame.
questions: Which animal has the best natural defense in your opinion? Why?
What do you think are some areas you may have to adapt to in the coming years? How does this make you feel?

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time they had to change their behavior to make it out of a rough situation. How did God give them Psalm 34-like courage or wisdom in this situation?

thoughts.verse: I prayed to the LORD, and he answered me. He freed me from all my fears. Those who look to him for help will be radiant with joy; no shadow of shame will darken their faces. In my desperation I prayed, and the LORD listened; he saved me from all my troubles. Psalms 34:4-6 Read through his verse slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a deep sense of peace in your heart regardless of what you are going through. Take one or two verses from Psalm 34 and turn it into a prayer.

days.title: Friends In Low Places
days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 22:1-2 David left Gath and escaped to the cave of Adullam. When his brothers and his father’s household heard about it, they went down to him there. All those who were in distress or in debt or discontented gathered around him, and he became their commander. About four hundred men were with him.
days.reflection: There is a verse in Romans chapter 8 that you have looked at a couple of times so far in the Primal Path. It's found in Romans 8 v 31 and it says this: “And we know that God causes everything to work together for the good of those who love God and are called according to his purpose for them.” This is one of the most hopeful and encouraging verses you can ever memorize and understand as you become a man. It’s not just some random verse written a long time ago to make you feel better when you are having a tough time. It’s a verse full of power. because it shows us that we are not alone in the worst seasons and moments of our lives. In fact, God may be working in them to prepare you for something amazing to happen. Remember Joseph in Shift 1 (Ease to Difficulty)? Even though he was sold into slavery by his brothers, God worked to get him into the most powerful place in the land so that he could save the lives of many. God used what was horrific to be helpful for so many. Well, you are going to see another example of that today. David is still on the run from King Saul, and he seems deeply discouraged. Instead of being king, like he was anointed to be, he is hiding in the cave. He has no throne, no people, and no army. But then something remarkable happens. Word begins to get out that David is there. And people begin to rally to him as their leader. First, his father and brothers come and join him. This is an amazing change of heart on their behalf. From a father who forgot him and brothers who opposed him, David now has their loyalty and support. Then, he begins to gather an army of outcasts who want to be his followers. They finally had a leader they can trust. They knew that David understood what it's like to be distressed, in debt, and discontent. So they follow him as their commander. When something goes really wrong, we call it a catastrophe. But what do you call it when something turns out really good? It's called a eucatastrophe. Author Skye Jethani says this: “J. R. R. Tolkien, the author of The Lord of the Rings, often employed a storytelling device he called eucatastrophe. A catastrophe is an unexpected evil, but Tolkien added the Greek prefix eu- meaning ‘good’ to express the unexpected appearing of goodness. He defined it as ‘the sudden happy turn in a story which pierces you with a joy that brings you to tears.’ Repeatedly in his stories the eucatastrophe occurs just as all hope appears to be lost. It is the moment the eagles swoop in for the rescue, the riders of Rohan arrive at the battle, or Gandalf the White appears with the breaking of the day." David’s hiding isn’t a disaster at all. It’s a eucatastrophe. In the worst turn of events, something amazing has happened. He has his family, he has an army, and he has God on his side. Remember to stay calm in the midst of a crisis and look for God to work. You never know how he will turn it for good. He did it for Joseph, Moses, and David, and he will do it for you if you trust him and are patient.
questions: Have you ever seen something that seemed bad at the time turn out to be good?
How do you think most guys make friends today? What do they tend to connect around?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time they have seen God work something together for good. What was it, and how did it turn out?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. In his kindness God called you to share in his eternal glory by means of Christ Jesus. So after you have suffered a little while, he will restore, support, and strengthen you, and he will place you on a firm foundation. All power to him forever! Amen.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in any of them?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to show you where he is working in your life right now. Thank him for the plans he has for you.

days.title: Coming To Terms With Change
days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 27:1-7 But David thought to himself, “One of these days I will be destroyed by the hand of Saul. The best thing I can do is to escape to the land of the Philistines. Then Saul will give up searching for me anywhere in Israel, and I will slip out of his hand.” So David and the six hundred men with him left and went over to Achish son of Maok king of Gath. David and his men settled in Gath with Achish. Each man had his family with him, and David had his two wives: Ahinoam of Jezreel and Abigail of Carmel, the widow of Nabal. When Saul was told that David had fled to Gath, he no longer searched for him. Then David said to Achish, “If I have found favor in your eyes, let a place be assigned to me in one of the country towns, that I may live there. Why should your servant live in the royal city with you?” So on that day Achish gave him Ziklag, and it has belonged to the kings of Judah ever since. David lived in Philistine territory a year and four months.
days.reflection: One of the most challenging things about growing up and learning how to deal with life is navigating change. We often wish things would always stay the same. But as we grow, we have to handle so much transition. Our friends change as people, our thoughts and desires change, our bodies change as we get older, and the world changes around us. But one of the hardest kinds of change to deal with is changes that you don’t want to happen. Having to change schools because your parents move, having to change friends because relationships break down, or changing locations because things get dangerous where you are. It can be hard to adjust, settle in, get your bearings, and thrive. David experienced this during these years of running and hiding from Saul. He was in the wilderness, in caves with his friends, on the run. He wasn’t able to be still and settle down. But in today’s reading, he settles into a new city, one that is given to him by his old enemies and new allies, the Philistines. But it was hard on David. His heart was to be with his own people, in his own land, and now he is stuck trying to make the best of things where he is. He has 600 men with him now in an army, plus all their families, and they have to learn to build their lives. God is teaching David resilience. One of the keys to handling change is learning to become resilient too. The Merriam-Webster dictionary defines resilience as “the ability to recover from or adjust easily to misfortune or change.” You are going to have to learn that you cannot control time. You cannot control God, and you cannot control everything that happens in your life. You are going to have to learn to surrender things to God, be resilient with what he allows, and keep growing through it all. Just like God prepared David when he was out with the sheep, God was preparing David through all of his running from Saul. God was preparing David to be king and lead with integrity. None of our trials are wasted with God. That’s why Romans 5:3-4 says, “We can rejoice, too, when we run into problems and trials, for we know that they help us develop endurance. And endurance develops strength of character, and character strengthens our confident hope of salvation.” David had to surrender his desire for comfort to God during these difficult years. But God was faithful. He matured David into the kind of man who could be trusted with power, one who would lead like a man after his own heart. And God will develop you through your teenage years if you focus on growth, not comfort. You are well on your way into manhood now. Embrace discomfort; that’s where your strength lies.
questions: Can you think of a change that has happened in your life recently that you didn’t like? What was it, and how was it hard?
What are 2 or 3 changes that guys your age look forward to? What are a couple of changes they don’t like?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how surrendering their desire for comfort and embracing growth instead has helped them as a man. Ask them to be as specific as possible.

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. The LORD directs the steps of the godly. He delights in every detail of their lives. Though they stumble, they will never fall, for the LORD holds them by the hand.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in any of them?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God that he is growing you up, training you, and maturing you, even if if’s hard. Thank him for his commitment to you.

extras: WEEK 5 MANSCHOOL SELF DEFENSE MAN SCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. - John Eldredge OPTION 1. ACTIVITY: SELF-DEFENSE In the modern world, we may find ourselves in positions where we have to defend ourselves from immediate danger. Why not take an introductory lesson in some kind of martial art this week, or bring someone over you know who does jiu-jitsu or MMA to give you an overview of self-defense? It’s a fun thing to do together, and young guys are often wanting to test their strengths. OPTION 2. WATCH DEFIANCE This is a gripping and true story of 3 Jewish brothers who had to flee to save their lives, and end up saving the lives of many more. Warning- it’s intense and R – Rated, so use discernment. But the movie embodies the ideas of what David went through in running from Saul very well. https://youtu.be/FVNR8PrtnQc OPTION 3. DINNER AND DISCUSSION Take your son out to one of his favorite restaurants, or for desserts and talk through what it means to deal with danger. You may want to talk through things that have happened in your life, or stuff going on in his life, or stories of danger and survival from your family history. You may want to talk about:
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
